title: The antiferromagnetic Chern insulator phase in the Kane-Mele-Hubbard model
authors: Bao-Qing Wang, Can Shao, Takami Tohyama, Hong-Gang Luo, Hantao Lu
abstract: The emergence of the antiferromagnetic (AFM) Chern insulator (AFCI) phase in the Kane-Mele-Hubbard (KMH) model with a finite sublattice potential is investigated. The AFCI, characterized by AFM correlations coexisting with quantized Hall conductance, has long raised the question of whether it can exist in the KMH model that respects time-reversal symmetry (TRS). Using exact diagonalization, we analyze the excitation gap, anisotropic AFM correlations along the $z$ axis and in the $xy$ plane, and the fidelity susceptibility under twisted boundary conditions, all of which provide consistent evidence for the AFCI phase. In particular, our numerical evaluation on the (spin) Chern number reveals a breakdown of adiabatic continuity in the twist-angle space, indicating an instability toward TRS breaking driven by Hubbard-induced AFM perturbations. A modified computational scheme is further proposed, which yields a robust quantized Chern number $C=1$ within this phase.
summary: This paper investigates the emergence of an antiferromagnetic Chern insulator phase in the Kane-Mele-Hubbard model. By using exact diagonalization and a new computational scheme to handle level crossings, the authors demonstrate that Hubbard interactions can drive a transition that breaks time-reversal symmetry. The results resolve long-standing discrepancies in the literature regarding the stability of this phase. This work is significant for understanding how electron correlations can induce topological transitions in systems with intrinsic spin-orbit coupling.
main_problem: The study investigates whether an antiferromagnetic Chern insulator (AFCI) phase can emerge in the Kane-Mele-Hubbard model, addressing the controversy between previous mean-field and quantum Monte Carlo results.
main_result: The authors provide evidence for the existence of an AFCI phase (C=1) where Hubbard-induced antiferromagnetic perturbations spontaneously break time-reversal symmetry, and they propose a modified numerical scheme to accurately calculate the quantized Chern number.
method: The study uses Exact Diagonalization (ED) on a 12A cluster, Twisted Boundary Conditions (TBC) to evaluate topological invariants, and Mean-Field theory to provide physical insight into the effective Dirac masses.
model_or_system: The Kane-Mele-Hubbard (KMH) model on a honeycomb lattice at half-filling, including nearest-neighbor hopping, intrinsic spin-orbit coupling, a staggered sublattice potential, and onsite repulsive Hubbard interaction.
key_observables: Chern number (C), spin Chern number (Cs), excitation gap, fidelity susceptibility, and spin-density-wave (SDW) and charge-density-wave (CDW) structure factors.
important_parameters: Hubbard interaction strength (U), sublattice potential (Delta_AB), and intrinsic spin-orbit coupling strength (t2).
main_assumptions: The study is limited by finite-size effects inherent to Exact Diagonalization on small clusters.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the ED phase diagram; Figure 2 displays the excitation gap and topological transitions; Figure 7 shows the phase diagram with mass imbalance; Figure 10 and 11 present Mean-Field phase diagrams and spin-resolved effective potentials.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem and model, presents the ED phase diagram and identification of the AFCI phase, details a modified numerical algorithm for Chern number calculation, explores the stability of the phase under mass imbalance, and provides Mean-Field analysis for physical interpretation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. ED Phase diagram of the KMH model (1) in the (U, ∆AB) plane with fixed t2 = 0.2.
FIG. 2. Contour plots of (a) the excitation gap ∆ex and (b) the z-component of the AFM structure factor Szz AFM, shown as functions of the Hubbard interaction U and the sublattice potential ∆AB.

FIG. 3. (a) CDW and SDW structure factors, SCDW, Szz AFM, and Sxy AFM, along with the excitation gap ∆ex and the single- particle charge gap ∆c. (b) Fidelity susceptibility χF with respect to twisted boundary conditions. All quantities are plotted as functions of U at fixed ∆AB = 4.0. The dashed vertical lines mark the phase boundaries determined from the topological invariant calculations.

FIG. 4. Evaluation of the Chern number C and spin Chern number Cs as functions of U at fixed ∆AB = 4.0. Panel (a) shows the results obtained using conventional methods, while panel (b) presents those obtained with the modified algorithm described in the text.

FIG. 5. (a) Excitation gap ∆ex and (b) lattice field strength ˜F [Eq. (12)] as functions of twist angles (θx, θy) under spin- independent TBCs. Dashed boundaries in (b) denote the swap region, where the Berry curvature is evaluated using the first exited state of ˆH(θ) instead of the ground state. See the main text for details. Parameters: ∆AB = 4.0 and U = 8.0.

FIG. 6. (a) Ground-state energy E0 and first excitation en- ergy E1, and (b) magnitude of the unnormalized U(1) link ˜U, defined as ˜U (θℓ, δθℓ) = |⟨Ψ (θℓ) |Ψ (θℓ+ δθℓ)⟩|, plotted as functions of θx along the antidiagonal line in θ space. “SP” denotes a singular point. Parameters: ∆AB = 4.0 and U = 8.0.
FIG. 7. ED phase diagram of the KMH Hamiltonian (1) with mass imbalance δ = 0.1 (16). Notably, without in- teraction (U = 0), the C = 1 phase appears in the range ∆AB ∈[0.95, 1.08].

FIG. 9. Evolution of the excitation gaps as functions of U under PBC and spin-dependent TBCs (10), denoted by ∆PBC ex and ∆TBC ex , respectively. The values of ∆TBC ex are obtained as the minimum excitation gap over the entire θ space. The sublattice potential is fixed at ∆AB = 0.5.

FIG. 8. Excitation gap ∆ex and lattice field strength ˜Fs as functions of the twisted angles (θx, θy) under spin-dependent TBCs (10), with sublattice potential fixed at ∆AB = 0.5. (a,b) Results at U = 7.0 (QSH phase); (c,d) U = 8.95 (near the phase boundary); (e,f) U = 11.0 (SDW phase). Calcula- tions are performed on a 100 × 100 grid in θ space.
FIG. 10. MF phase diagram of the KMH model (1) in the (U, ∆AB) plane with fixed t2 = 0.2, obtained on a 30 × 30 lattice.
